content.text: ```json { "module": "ui-design-system", "scores": { "visual_polish": 62, "domain_depth": 38, "data_richness": 35, "interaction_quality": 55, "ai_integration": 15, "mobile_first": 42, "performance": 50, "typescript_quality": 58, "error_handling": 60, "realtime": 30, "accessibility": 35, "security": 45, "differentiation": 40, "enterprise_readiness": 45 }, "overall_score": 44, "issues": [ { "id": "ui-design-system-C5-001", "severity": "critical", "dimension": "accessibility", "title": "Primitives components missing ARIA roles and labels on interactive elements", "file": "src/components/Primitives.tsx", "description": "MetricBox, Card, Btn, Tag, and table row components lack aria-label, role, and aria-live attributes. Status badges have no screen reader text. Color-only status indicators fail WCAG 2.1 AA. Every interactive primitive must have accessible names and keyboard focus indicators per Global Acceptance Standards.", "prompt": "In the file src/components/Primitives.tsx, make these specific changes: In the MetricBox component, add role='region' and aria-label={label} to the outer container div. In the Btn component, ensure the button element has aria-busy={loading} when in loading state and aria-disabled={disabled} when disabled. In the Tag component, add role='status' and aria-label that combines the tag text with its color meaning (e.g. 'Open, status: active'). In any StatusDot or status indicator component, add a visually hidden span with sr-only class containing the status text so screen readers announce it (e.g. {statusText}). BEFORE: Interactive primitives have no ARIA attributes; status dots convey meaning through color only; buttons in loading state have no aria-busy. AFTER: All primitives have proper ARIA roles and labels; status indicators include visually hidden text; buttons communicate loading/disabled state to assistive technology. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors." }, { "id": "ui-design-system-C5-002", "severity": "critical", "dimension": "mobile_first", "title": "Sidebar lacks mobile bottom navigation and 44px touch targets", "file": "src/components/Sidebar.tsx", "description": "The sidebar renders as a fixed left panel on all screen sizes with no bottom navigation on mobile. Touch targets for nav items are smaller than the required 44x44px minimum per Apple HIG and PAGE_ACCEPTANCE_CRITERIA. On screens under 768px, the sidebar should collapse to a bottom tab bar showing the 5 most important sections (Dashboard, RFIs, Daily Log, Punch List, Schedule).", "prompt": "In the file src/components/Sidebar.tsx, make these specific changes: Add a useEffect or useState hook that tracks window width using matchMedia('(max-width: 768px)'). When isMobile is true, render a fixed bottom navigation bar instead of the left sidebar. The bottom bar should be 64px tall, positioned fixed at bottom:0, left:0, right:0 with zIndex 1000 and background colors.sidebarBg. Include exactly 5 navigation items: Dashboard, RFIs, Daily Log, Punch List, and Schedule, each with their Lucide icon (16px) above a label (10px font). Each touch target must be minHeight:'44px' and minWidth:'44px' with display:'flex', flexDirection:'column', alignItems:'center', justifyContent:'center'. The active item should have color: colors.primary (orange). Add a 'More' item that opens a slide-up sheet with remaining nav items. When isMobile is true, do NOT render the desktop sidebar. BEFORE: Sidebar renders as 240px fixed left panel on all screen sizes; nav items have small click targets; no bottom navigation on mobile. AFTER: On screens under 768px, a bottom tab bar with 5 key sections replaces the sidebar; all touch targets are 44x44px minimum; desktop sidebar unchanged. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors." }, { "id": "ui-design-system-C5-003", "severity": "high", "dimension": "interaction_quality", "title": "No global command palette (Cmd+K) for keyboard power users", "file": "src/components/CommandPalette.tsx", "description": "The design standards require a Cmd+K command palette for global search, navigation, and quick actions. This is missing entirely. Linear, Stripe, and every modern productivity tool has this. Without it, keyboard-only users cannot navigate efficiently and the app feels dated compared to competitors. PAGE_ACCEPTANCE_CRITERIA specifies Cmd+K for command palette as a global keyboard shortcut.", "prompt": "Create the file src/components/CommandPalette.tsx with these specific contents: Build a React component CommandPalette that renders a modal overlay when open. It should listen for Cmd+K (Mac) or Ctrl+K (Windows) globally via useEffect on the document keydown event. The modal should have a search input at the top (40px height, 14px font, no border, full width, autofocus). Below the input, show a filtered list of navigation items matching the search query against routeLabels from Breadcrumbs.tsx patterns. Each result item should be 48px height, hoverable with colors.backgroundHover, showing the Lucide icon + page name + keyboard shortcut hint (e.g. 'G then D' for Dashboard). Pressing Enter on a focused result navigates using react-router useNavigate(). Pressing Escape closes the palette. Use a fixed overlay with background:'rgba(0,0,0,0.5)', centered card with maxWidth:'560px', maxHeight:'400px', borderRadius:'12px', background:colors.cardBg, boxShadow:shadows.xl. Filter results using case-insensitive includes on the query. Show 'No results' empty state. Export the component and render it in App.tsx at the root level so it is available on every page. BEFORE: No command palette exists; no Cmd+K shortcut; no keyboard navigation between pages. AFTER: Cmd+K opens a searchable command palette; users can navigate to any page by typing; Escape closes it; results are keyboard navigable with arrow keys. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors." }, { "id": "ui-design-system-C5-004", "severity": "high", "dimension": "error_handling", "title": "Toast system lacks keyboard dismiss and ARIA live region announcement", "file": "src/components/ContextMenu.tsx", "description": "The toast system in ContextMenu.tsx renders toasts without an aria-live='polite' container, so screen readers never announce new toasts. Error toasts with null duration lack a visible countdown or progress indicator showing when they will auto-dismiss. The toast container div needs role='alert' for error severity and role='status' for info/success. The Escape key handler only works for the last toast, not the focused one.", "prompt": "In the file src/components/ContextMenu.tsx, make these specific changes: In the ToastProvider component's render output, wrap the toast list container div with aria-live='polite' and aria-atomic='false'. In the ToastEntry component, add role='alert' when toast.severity is 'error' or 'warning', and role='status' when severity is 'success' or 'info'. For error toasts (which have null auto-dismiss duration), add a visible countdown timer display showing '60s' that counts down, rendered as a small text next to the dismiss button with fontSize:'11px' and color:style.text. Change the Escape key handler: instead of only the isLast toast responding to Escape, make each ToastEntry track its own focus state and respond to Escape when it contains document.activeElement. Add aria-label='Dismiss notification' to the dismiss button inside each toast. BEFORE: Toast container has no aria-live region; screen readers do not announce new toasts; error toasts show no visible auto-dismiss countdown; Escape only closes the last toast. AFTER: Toast container has aria-live='polite'; each toast has appropriate role (alert/status); error toasts show a visible countdown; Escape works on the focused toast; dismiss button has aria-label. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors." }, { "id": "ui-design-system-C5-005", "severity": "high", "dimension": "visual_polish", "title": "Skeleton loader components missing for consistent loading states across all pages", "file": "src/components/SkeletonLoaders.tsx", "description": "Global Acceptance Standards require skeleton loaders matching final layout geometry on every page. Currently there is no shared skeleton component library. Each page either shows a spinner, blank content, or implements its own inconsistent loading state. A centralized set of skeleton primitives (SkeletonCard, SkeletonTable, SkeletonMetricRow, SkeletonText) with the standard pulse animation is needed for all pages to import.", "prompt": "Create the file src/components/SkeletonLoaders.tsx with these specific contents: Export four skeleton primitives. 1) SkeletonBox: accepts width, height, borderRadius props, renders a div with background:colors.border, the specified dimensions, and a CSS keyframe pulse animation (opacity oscillating between 0.3 and 0.7 over 1.5s ease-in-out infinite) applied via a style tag or inline animation. 2) SkeletonMetricCard: renders a Card-shaped div (borderRadius:'12px', padding:'24px', border:'1px solid '+colors.border, background:colors.cardBg) containing a SkeletonBox for the icon (24x24, borderRadius:'6px'), a SkeletonBox for the label (80x12), and a SkeletonBox for the value (120x28). 3) SkeletonTableRow: renders a div with display:'flex', gap:'16px', height:'48px', alignItems:'center', padding:'0 16px', containing 4 SkeletonBox elements of varying widths (40px, flex:2, flex:1, 80px) and height 14px each. 4) SkeletonTable: accepts rowCount prop (default 8), renders a header row of SkeletonBox items plus rowCount SkeletonTableRow components. All components use colors from '../styles/theme'. Add the pulse animation using a React useEffect that injects a style tag with @keyframes skeletonPulse if not already present. BEFORE: No shared skeleton components; pages show inconsistent loading states or blank content. AFTER: Four reusable skeleton primitives available for import; all use consistent pulse animation and theme colors; geometry matches common page layouts (metric cards, table rows). After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors." } ], "invented_features": [ { "title": "Construction Color Token System with Semantic Meaning", "description": "Extend the theme with construction-specific semantic color tokens: tradeElectrical, tradePlumbing, tradeHVAC, tradeConcrete, tradeSteel, tradeDrywall etc. mapped to consistent colors across punch list, schedule, crew, and directory views. This eliminates ad-hoc color assignment and ensures a superintendent instantly recognizes trade associations by color anywhere in the app." } ], "competitive_intel": "Procore's UI design system is functional but dated, relying on a 2018-era component library with heavy borders and dense layouts. Fieldwire has superior mobile-first primitives with swipe gestures built into their component layer. Linear and Stripe set the bar for command palettes and keyboard navigation that SiteSync currently lacks entirely. The missing Cmd+K palette and absent mobile bottom nav are the two biggest gaps versus modern SaaS competitors. Skeleton loaders and ARIA compliance are table stakes that SiteSync falls below.", "summary": "The UI Design System module scores 44 overall, held back primarily by missing accessibility infrastructure (no ARIA on primitives, no screen reader announcements on toasts), absent mobile-first navigation (no bottom tab bar, undersized touch targets), and lack of keyboard power-user features (no command palette). The toast system and breadcrumb components show solid engineering patterns but lack the accessibility and responsive layers required by the Global Acceptance Standards. The five fixes target: ARIA attributes on all primitive components, mobile bottom navigation replacing the sidebar on small screens, a Cmd+K command palette for global navigation, toast accessibility with live regions and visible countdown, and a shared skeleton loader library to eliminate inconsistent loading states. These changes directly address the three lowest-scoring dimensions (accessibility 35, real-time/data-richness 30-35, mobile-first 42) and will move the module toward the 60+ range needed before Architect mode features make sense." } ```
